sections.title: Scoring
rules: A game is played to 11 points. A player must win by at least 2 points.
A match is typically best of 5 or best of 7 games.
Each player serves 2 points in a row, then the serve switches. At deuce (10-10), the serve alternates every point.
A point is scored when the opponent fails to make a legal return, or commits a fault.

sections.title: Service Rules
rules: The ball must rest on an open palm before being tossed at least 16cm (about 6 inches) vertically.
The server must strike the ball so it bounces on their side first, then the opponent's side.
The ball must be visible to the opponent and the umpire at all times during the serve.
In doubles, the serve must go diagonally from the server's right half to the receiver's right half.

sections.title: Rally Rules
rules: After the serve, players must alternate hitting the ball, letting it bounce once on their side.
The ball must pass over or around the net assembly and land on the opponent's side.
If the ball touches the net during a rally but lands on the correct side, play continues (net/edge balls are valid).
A let is called if the ball touches the net on a serve but lands in the correct area.

sections.title: Equipment Regulations
rules: The racket can be any size, shape, or weight, but the blade must be flat and rigid, with at least 85% natural wood.
One side of the racket must be red and the other black.
The ball must be 40mm in diameter, weigh 2.7g, and be made of celluloid or similar plastic material.
The table must be 274cm long, 152.5cm wide, and 76cm high, with a 15.25cm high net.

sections.title: Doubles Rules
rules: Partners must alternate hitting the ball during a rally.
The serve must go from the server's right half-court to the receiver's right half-court.
After each game, the receiving pair switches who receives first.
At the change of serve in the final game, the pair due to receive next must also switch receiver.

sections.title: Common Faults
rules: Touching the table surface with your free hand during a rally results in a point for the opponent.
Moving the table during play is a fault.
If a player strikes the ball twice in succession, the opponent scores a point.
Obstructing the ball (volleying before it bounces on your side) loses the point.
